title: 27 Nehase — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly
saints: The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Crucifixion
St. Pimen the Great (Abba Poemen), Desert Father
narrative:
On the twenty-seventh day of every month, the Ethiopian Church pauses in its ordinary commemorations to stand once more before the Cross. The monthly Crucifixion remembrance is among the most distinctive and theologically profound features of the Ethiopian liturgical calendar: it refuses to let the Passion recede into the past, insisting that the Cross is not a completed event but a perpetually present reality, the axis of all time, the still point around which every month revolves. To stand before the Cross on the twenty-seventh of Nehase, days after the Transfiguration and the Assumption, is to be reminded of what those glorious feasts cost and what they are built upon. The light of Tabor shines from a face marked by thorns. The glorified body of the Virgin is a body drawn from the womb of a people who waited through darkness. Every mountain of glory has Golgotha somewhere in its foundation.

Abba Poemen — Pimen the Great — was one of the most celebrated of all the desert fathers, whose sayings fill a substantial portion of the Apophthegmata Patrum and whose wisdom has shaped the contemplative tradition in every subsequent era. His name means 'shepherd,' and he was indeed a shepherd of souls in the fullest sense, gathering around him a community of disciples that was among the most luminous of the entire Egyptian desert. But his wisdom was hard-won: Poemen was not a saint who found the ascetic life easy. He suffered, he struggled, he wept, and out of that wrestling came counsel of the most practical and penetrating kind. 'Whatever you do, do it not with the boldness of one who knows, but with the humility of one who is learning.' The monthly Crucifixion and Abba Poemen belong together because both insist on the necessity of what the world calls failure as the precondition of what heaven calls glory.

St. Phanourios the Great Martyr is an interesting figure in the calendar: his relics were discovered in Rhodes in the fifteenth century, long after his martyrdom, and the rediscovery of a forgotten saint was treated by the Church as a new revelation of grace — as if God had preserved him in hiddenness for a specific moment and purpose. He has become the patron of those who seek lost things, and his intercessions are sought not only for lost objects but for lost people, lost paths, lost causes. On the twenty-seventh day of Nehase, with the monthly Crucifixion as the day's anchor, Phanourios speaks to the nature of redemption itself: Christ on the Cross is the God who came to find what was lost, to recover what had been hidden, to restore what had fallen. Every lost thing that is found by Phanourios's intercession is a small sacrament of the great Recovery.

The monthly Crucifixion, Abba Poemen, and Phanourios together make of this twenty-seventh day a day of depth and searching. The fast of Filseta has passed; the feasts of Tekle Haymanot and Joshua and the other great saints have been celebrated. Now the month draws toward its close, and the Cross stands again at the center: not as a rebuke but as a compass, orienting everything that has come before and everything that follows, reminding the Church that the glory it has celebrated is always a cruciform glory, always risen from the place of dying, always most true at the point where it is most costly.
